perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned or delivered to the Disclosing Party, with all copies made thereof, in whatever form, if the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party may be retained indefinitely by the Receiving Party, even if the Disclosing Party requests return or delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information, even upon request, contradicts the spirit of trade secret law and could be seen as misappropriation under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - California Civil Code § 3426.1
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. Retaining confidential information indefinitely against the Disclosing Party's express request would be considered a breach of duty and an unlawful use of the information, potentially violating CUTSA's prohibition against misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent and legal protections afforded to trade secrets. By allowing the Receiving Party to retain confidential information indefinitely, even after the Disclosing Party requests its return, the changed text enables potential misappropriation as defined in California Civil Code § 3426.1. Specifically, the original contract ensures return of confidential information upon request, aligning with the duty to maintain secrecy. The modified text removes this obligation, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to 'use a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which falls under misappropriation if the original acquisition carried a duty to maintain secrecy, as stated in snippet 1, CIV 3426.1 (b)(2).

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law as the governing law might conflict with U.S. public policy, especially if the contract involves intellectual property or technology subject to U.S. export controls or sanctions.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 730 and the broader EAR regulate the export and re-export of goods, software, and technology. Choosing North Korean law raises concerns because enforcing a contract under such jurisdiction could lead to violations of U.S. export controls and sanctions against North Korea, as it might compel actions that are prohibited under U.S. law to prevent sensitive technology from reaching North Korea.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Japanese law, while the changed text specifies North Korean law. The Law Explanation states that enforcing a contract under North Korean jurisdiction could violate U.S. export controls and sanctions. The Perturbation explanation adds that this is especially true when it involves intellectual property or technology subject to U.S. export controls or sanctions. Snippet 1 references 15 CFR Part 730, which regulates exports and re-exports. Therefore, using North Korean law creates a contradiction by potentially forcing actions illegal under U.S. law, as applying North Korean law could compel actions violating export controls, which falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.
